text:
# 📚 TPM Prep Library - Navigation Index

> **Your complete offline TPM study library.** Start HERE every time.
> Last updated: June 2026 | 14 files total | Fully offline

---

## 🎯 How to Use This Library

| If you want to... | Open this file first |
|-------------------|---------------------|
| Know what to study TODAY | `01_TPM_24_Week_Consolidated_Study_Plan.md` |
| Navigate everything quickly | You're here! |
| See the big-picture learning path | `ROADMAP.md` |
| Cram before an interview in 15 min | `11_TPM_Quick_Reference_Cheat_Sheet.md` |
| Look up a term you forgot | `12_TPM_Glossary.md` |

---

## 📁 File-by-File Guide

### 🧭 Master Plan & Career Strategy

| # | File | Pages | What's Inside | Start Here If... |
|---|------|-------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | `core/TPM_Transition_24_Week_Playbook_v5.md` | ~2500 lines | Your complete career roadmap: profile, resume, LinkedIn strategy, application plan, networking templates, salary negotiation playbook, STAR story bank, 6 TPM templates (RAID Log, Program Charter, Status Report, Dependency Tracker, Risk Register, Executive 1-Pager), and 2 case study shells (Sally & CDL) | You want your PERSONAL career plan |
| 2 | `01_TPM_24_Week_Consolidated_Study_Plan.md` | ~886 lines | Day-by-day study schedule for 24 weeks. Tells you exactly which file to read on which day in what order | You don't know where to START today |

### 🏗️ Core TPM Frameworks & Knowledge

| # | File | Lines | What's Inside | Best For... |
|---|------|-------|---------------|-------------|
| 3 | `02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md` | ~2120 | 9 frameworks explained from zero: Agile, Scrum, Waterfall, SAFe, RAID Log, RACI, OKRs, MoSCoW, WBS. Each has: definition, principles, step-by-step, example, case study | ABSOLUTE BEGINNERS to TPM frameworks |
| 4 | `03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md` | ~1615 | 6 competencies: Program Scoping, Stakeholder Management, Risk Management, Cross-Functional Communications, Delivery & Execution, Influence Without Authority. Each has: definition, skills, pitfalls, frameworks, realistic example, case study | Deep-diving into specific TPM skills |
| 5 | `04_TPM_Planning_Estimation_Guide.md` | ~1735 | 8 planning methods: Roadmapping, Milestones, Capacity Planning, T-Shirt Sizing, PERT, Critical Path Analysis, Phased Delivery, Case Studies | Learning HOW to estimate and plan |
| 6 | `05_TPM_Risk_Dependency_Management.md` | ~1504 | 5 risk domains: Risk Identification, Risk Mitigation, Dependency Mapping, Escalation, Change Management. Each has framework + example + playbook | Mastering RISK & DEPENDENCY management |
| 7 | `06_TPM_Metrics_Execution_Tracking.md` | ~1435 | 6 metrics frameworks: KPIs, OKR Tracking, Burn-Down Charts, Velocity/Throughput, SLAs, Program Health Dashboards | Tracking whether your program is ON TRACK |
| 8 | `07_TPM_Stakeholder_Communication_Guide.md` | ~1299 | 5 communication domains: Stakeholder Mapping, Executive Reporting, Steering Committees, Conflict Resolution, Negotiation | Communicating with STAKEHOLDERS and executives |
| 9 | `08_TPM_Tools_Artifacts_Guide.md` | ~1711 | 6 tools: JIRA, Confluence, Miro, Program Charters, Status Decks, Runbooks | Using TOOLS that TPMs use daily |

### 🔬 Specialized & Advanced

| # | File | Lines | What's Inside | Best For... |
|---|------|-------|---------------|-------------|
| 10 | `09_TPM_Technical_Depth_Research_Report.md` | ~529 | 7 technical domains: System Design, APIs, Cloud, GenAI/LLM, CI/CD, Architecture Reviews, Infrastructure Scaling. Each has: depth framework, impact analysis, examples, case studies | Understanding what TECHNICAL depth you need |
| 11 | `10_TPM_Interview_Prep_Guide.md` | ~1075 | STAR stories (5 built from YOUR projects), behavioral questions, program design framework, estimation framework, conflict scenarios, questions to ask, salary strategy, stage-by-stage guide | INTERVIEW preparation |

### ⚡ Quick Reference & Support

| # | File | Lines | What's Inside | Best For... |
|---|------|-------|---------------|-------------|
| 12 | `11_TPM_Quick_Reference_Cheat_Sheet.md` | ~289 | All frameworks in 1–3 sentences each. RAG, RAID, RACI, OKRs, MoSCoW, PERT, CP, escalation, negotiation, India objection scripts, salary numbers | LAST-MINUTE review before interviews |
| 13 | `12_TPM_Glossary.md` | ~435 | A–Z glossary of every TPM term. Each term has 1-sentence definition + "why it matters" | Looking up terms you FORGOT |
| 14 | `ROADMAP.md` | - | ASCII visual of the entire 24-week learning path | Seeing the BIG PICTURE |

### 📊 Spreadsheet

| File | What's Inside |
|------|---------------|
| `Learning_TPgM_Transition_FINAL.xlsx` | Excel tracker - may contain timeline data, salary projections, application tracking |

## 📖 Study Order Recommendation

```
Month 1-2: Foundation
  START HERE → 24_Week_Study_Plan.md
  Then read: Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md (9 frameworks)
  Then read: Core_Competencies.md (6 competencies)
  Reference: Glossary.md (as needed)

Month 3-4: Deep Dive
  Read: Planning_Estimation_Guide.md
  Read: Risk_Dependency_Management.md
  Read: Metrics_Execution_Tracking.md
  Read: Stakeholder_Communication.md
  Read: Tools_Artifacts_Guide.md

Month 5-6: Application & Interview
  Read: Technical_Depth_Research.md
  Build: core/v5.md Section 19 (Sally + CDL case studies)
  Read: Interview_Prep_Guide.md
  Cram: Quick_Reference_Cheat_Sheet.md
  Refer: core/v5.md for your personal plan
```

# 📅 TPM 24-Week Consolidated Study Plan

> **Purpose:** A week-by-week, day-by-day plan that tells you exactly what to study, in what order, using only the files in this directory. No internet required.
>
> **Daily time:** 2 hours (adjustable - complete the week's work regardless)
> **Weekly time:** ~14 hours
> **Total program:** ~336 hours across 24 weeks
>
> **How to use this plan:** Each week has daily assignments. Check off items as you complete them. Revisit any week you find difficult before moving on.

---

## How the 24 Weeks Are Organized

| Phase | Weeks | Focus |
|-------|-------|-------|
| **Phase 1: Foundation** | 1–6 | Core frameworks, vocabulary, and TPM mindset |
| **Phase 2: Deep Dive** | 7–12 | Each TPM competency area explored in depth |
| **Phase 3: Application** | 13–18 | Case studies, practical application, portfolio building |
| **Phase 4: Interview Prep** | 19–24 | Stories, mock interviews, final preparation |

---

## PHASE 1 - Foundation (Weeks 1–6)

### Week 1: What Is a TPM?

**Goal:** Understand the role, its value, and how it differs from PM and EM.

**Day 1 - Role Definition (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the Executive Summary of `core/TPM_Transition_24_Week_Playbook_v5.md` (Section 1)
- [ ] Read Section 2: "Phase 1 - TPM Foundation" from v5.md
- [ ] Write a 1-paragraph answer: "What does a TPM do that a PM and an EM do not?"

**Day 2 - TPM vs. Related Roles (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "Six Core Competencies" introduction from `03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md` (first ~10 pages)
- [ ] Create a comparison table: TPM vs. PM vs. EM vs. Scrum Master
- [ ] Read your personal profile in v5.md Section 1 - note your strengths

**Day 3 - The TPM Mindset (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the first competency: **Program Scoping** from 03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md
- [ ] Pay special attention to: "Core Principles" and "Common Pitfalls"
- [ ] Write down: "Which of these pitfalls have I seen in my own work?"

**Day 4 - Stakeholder Management Intro (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the first section of `07_TPM_Stakeholder_Communication_Guide.md`: Stakeholder Mapping (the framework)
- [ ] Practice mapping the stakeholders for one of your current projects
- [ ] Use the Power-Interest Grid from the guide

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Re-read your notes from the week
- [ ] Write a 1-page summary: "What I learned about TPM this week"
- [ ] Start your Wins Journal (see v5.md Section 2 - Internal Conversion Track)
- [ ] **Action:** Create a RAID Log for Sally or CDL (use template in v5.md Section 18.2)

---

### Week 2: Agile & Scrum Fundamentals

**Goal:** Master the core delivery frameworks that TPMs use daily.

**Day 1 - Agile Philosophy (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "Agile" section from `02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md`
- [ ] Understand the 4 values and 12 principles
- [ ] Answer: "Why does Agile work for software but not for construction?"

**Day 2 - Scrum in Depth (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "Scrum" section from 02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md
- [ ] Memorize: 3 roles, 5 events, 3 artifacts
- [ ] Answer: "What's the difference between Agile and Scrum?"

**Day 3 - Waterfall & When to Use It (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "Waterfall" section from 02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md
- [ ] Compare: When would you choose Waterfall over Agile?
- [ ] Learn the Change Control Process

**Day 4 - SAFe for Scale (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "SAFe" section from 02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md
- [ ] Understand: ART, PI Planning, Release Train Engineer
- [ ] Answer: "When is SAFe necessary vs. overkill?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Review and update your RAID Log for Sally/CDL (now running for 2 weeks)
- [ ] Re-read your Agile/Scrum notes
- [ ] Quiz yourself: "Explain Scrum to someone who has never heard of it" (record on camera)
- [ ] **Action:** Send your first Executive Update template (v5.md Section 18.6) to your manager/team lead - frame it as a practice exercise

---

### Week 3: Core TPM Frameworks I - RAID, RACI, OKRs

**Goal:** Master the three most-used TPM frameworks.

**Day 1 - RAID Log (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "RAID Log" section from 02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md
- [ ] Also read the Risk Identification section from `05_TPM_Risk_Dependency_Management.md`
- [ ] Review your live RAID Log from Weeks 1–2 - what's missing?
- [ ] Practice: Identify 5 risks, 3 assumptions, 2 issues, 4 dependencies for a hypothetical program

**Day 2 - RACI Matrix (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "RACI Matrix" section from 02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md
- [ ] Create a RACI matrix for a recent cross-team project you were on
- [ ] Answer: "What's the difference between Responsible and Accountable?"
- [ ] Practice: The "only one A per task" rule - why does this matter?

**Day 3 - OKRs (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "OKRs" section from 02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md
- [ ] Also read "OKR Tracking" from `06_TPM_Metrics_Execution_Tracking.md`
- [ ] Write 3 OKRs for a hypothetical 3-month program
- [ ] Answer: "Why should OKRs be ambitious (70% = success) rather than guaranteed?"

**Day 4 - MoSCoW & WBS (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "MoSCoW" and "WBS" sections from 02_TPM_Frameworks_Beginners_Guide.md
- [ ] Practice: Take a large initiative and break it down using WBS
- [ ] Practice: Categorize 10 features using MoSCoW
- [ ] Answer: "How do MoSCoW and WBS work together?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Update your RAID Log (Week 3 - it's now running)
- [ ] Create a RACI matrix for one of your real projects
- [ ] Write 3 OKRs for yourself for the next month
- [ ] **Action:** Send your second Executive Update (now you have 2 in writing - this is portfolio evidence)

---

### Week 4: Frameworks II - Planning & Estimation

**Goal:** Learn how TPMs plan work and estimate timelines.

**Day 1 - Roadmapping (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Roadmapping" from `04_TPM_Planning_Estimation_Guide.md`
- [ ] Create a roadmap for a 6-month program you know well (Sally or CDL)
- [ ] Practice: Explain "Now / Next / Later" to a non-technical stakeholder

**Day 2 - Milestones & Capacity Planning (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Milestones" and "Capacity Planning" from 04_TPM_Planning_Estimation_Guide.md
- [ ] Calculate: what's the effective capacity of a team of 6 engineers in a 2-week sprint? (Include: meetings, context-switching, unplanned work)
- [ ] Practice: Set 5 milestones for a program you know

**Day 3 - T-Shirt Sizing & PERT (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "T-Shirt Sizing" and "PERT" from 04_TPM_Planning_Estimation_Guide.md
- [ ] Practice: Size 8 features using T-Shirt sizes (team exercise in your head)
- [ ] Practice: Calculate PERT expected duration for a task with O=3, M=7, P=15
- [ ] Answer: "Why is a single-point estimate almost always wrong?"

**Day 4 - Critical Path & Phased Delivery (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Critical Path Analysis" and "Phased Delivery" from 04_TPM_Planning_Estimation_Guide.md
- [ ] Map the critical path for a program you know
- [ ] Answer: "If a non-critical-path task slips by 3 days, does the program slip? Why or why not?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Review your live RAID Log (Week 4 running)
- [ ] Add a critical path analysis to your Sally/CDL notes
- [ ] Quiz yourself on estimation frameworks: when to use T-Shirt vs. PERT vs. detailed estimation
- [ ] **Action:** Export your RAID Log as a PDF - this is now a 4-week-old live artifact for your portfolio

---

### Week 5: Risk & Dependency Management

**Goal:** Become proficient at identifying, mitigating, and escalating risks.

**Day 1 - Risk Identification in Depth (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the full "Risk Identification" section from `05_TPM_Risk_Dependency_Management.md`
- [ ] Run a mental pre-mortem on a current or past project
- [ ] Practice: Identify 15+ risks across the 5 domains (Technical, Organizational, Dependency, Timeline, External)

**Day 2 - Risk Mitigation (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the full "Risk Mitigation" section from 05_TPM_Risk_Dependency_Management.md
- [ ] For each risk you identified yesterday, choose a mitigation strategy (Avoid/Mitigate/Transfer/Accept/Escalate)
- [ ] Calculate residual risk scores
- [ ] Practice: "A key engineer might leave. What's your mitigation plan?"

**Day 3 - Dependency Mapping (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the full "Dependency Mapping" section from 05_TPM_Risk_Dependency_Management.md
- [ ] Draw a dependency map for a multi-team program you know
- [ ] Identify the critical path dependencies
- [ ] Practice: Assign dependency owners (requesting + providing)

**Day 4 - Escalation & Change Management (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Escalation" and "Change Management" from 05_TPM_Risk_Dependency_Management.md
- [ ] Memorize: The 4-Sentence Escalation structure
- [ ] Practice: Write an escalation email for a hypothetical scenario using the 4-sentence format
- [ ] Answer: "What's the difference between L1, L2, L3, and L4 escalations?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Review and update your live RAID Log (Week 5 running)
- [ ] Write a 4-sentence escalation for a real or simulated issue
- [ ] Quiz yourself: "What's in each section of a RAID log? What's the difference between a risk and an issue?"
- [ ] **Action:** Add a dependency map sketch to your portfolio

---

### Week 6: Stakeholder Communication & Executive Reporting

**Goal:** Master the communication skills that separate great TPMs from average ones.

**Day 1 - Stakeholder Mapping Deep Dive (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Re-read "Stakeholder Mapping" from `07_TPM_Stakeholder_Communication_Guide.md`
- [ ] Do a full stakeholder mapping exercise: identify 15+ stakeholders, map on Power-Interest Grid
- [ ] Create an engagement plan for each Key Player
- [ ] Answer: "Who is a stakeholder you've been under-engaging?"

**Day 2 - Executive Reporting (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Executive Reporting" from 07_TPM_Stakeholder_Communication_Guide.md
- [ ] Memorize: The 3-Sentence Executive Update structure
- [ ] Practice: Write 3 different executive updates (green, amber, red scenarios)
- [ ] Answer: "What information does an executive need that a team lead doesn't?"

**Day 3 - Steering Committees (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Steering Committees" from 07_TPM_Stakeholder_Communication_Guide.md
- [ ] Design a steering committee: who's on it, how often, what's the agenda structure
- [ ] Practice: Write a pre-read document for a steering committee
- [ ] Answer: "Why should status updates NOT be presented in steering committee meetings?"

**Day 4 - Conflict Resolution & Negotiation (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Conflict Resolution" and "Negotiation" from 07_TPM_Stakeholder_Communication_Guide.md
- [ ] Memorize: The Interest-Based Resolution approach (separate positions from interests)
- [ ] Practice: Role-play a conflict between two team leads who want the same engineer
- [ ] Answer: "What's the difference between a position and an interest?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review & Phase 1 Wrap (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Full review of Weeks 1–6 notes
- [ ] Quiz yourself on ALL frameworks learned so far:
  - RAID Log, RACI, OKRs, MoSCoW, WBS, T-Shirt Sizing, PERT
  - Critical Path, Phased Delivery, Risk Mitigation Strategies (5 types)
  - 4-Sentence Escalation, Power-Interest Grid, 3-Sentence Executive Update
- [ ] Update your live RAID Log (Week 6 - now a 6-week-old artifact)
- [ ] Write a self-assessment: "What am I strongest at? What needs more work?"
- [ ] **Celebrate:** You've completed the foundation phase. 🎉

---

## PHASE 2 - Deep Dive (Weeks 7–12)

### Week 7: Six Core Competencies - Program Scoping & Stakeholder Management

**Goal:** Deep understanding of the first two TPM competencies.

**Day 1 - Program Scoping (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Re-read "Program Scoping" from `03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md`
- [ ] Study the CloudSync Technologies case study in detail
- [ ] Practice: Write a program charter for a hypothetical program (1 page)

**Day 2 - Scoping Workshop Design (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Learn the MoSCoW prioritization technique from the case study
- [ ] Design a 2-hour scoping workshop agenda
- [ ] Practice: Facilitate a simulated scoping conversation (role-play in your head)

**Day 3 - Stakeholder Management in Depth (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Re-read "Stakeholder Management" from 03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md
- [ ] Study the MedFlow Health case study - pay attention to decision rights
- [ ] Create a decision rights document for a hypothetical program

**Day 4 - Stakeholder Engagement Plans (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Create engagement plans for 5 stakeholders (real or simulated)
- [ ] Practice: "How would you engage a skeptical senior engineer?"
- [ ] Answer: "What do you do when a stakeholder with low interest suddenly becomes high interest?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Compare the scoping and stakeholder frameworks from the Deep Dive with what you learned in Weeks 1–6
- [ ] Update your portfolio with lessons from the CloudSync and MedFlow case studies
- [ ] **Action:** Review and refine your Wins Journal (should have 7+ entries now)

---

### Week 8: Six Core Competencies - Risk Management

**Goal:** Deep understanding of the third TPM competency.

**Day 1 - Risk Identification Deep Dive (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Re-read "Risk Management" from `03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md`
- [ ] Study the BuildRight Construction case study in detail
- [ ] Practice: Run a pre-mortem on a current or past project - write down everything that could cause failure

**Day 2 - Probability-Impact Matrix (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Master the P×I scoring system from the Deep Dive
- [ ] Score 15+ risks using the 3×3 matrix
- [ ] Practice: "A risk with P=3 and I=3 scores 9. What does this mean for action?"

**Day 3 - Mitigation Plan Design (2 hrs)**
- [ ] For the top 5 risks from your pre-mortem, design detailed mitigation plans
- [ ] Each plan needs: strategy, concrete actions, owner, due date, residual score
- [ ] Practice: "How do you know when a risk has been adequately mitigated?"

**Day 4 - Risk Monitoring Cadence (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Design a risk review cadence: weekly team review, monthly stakeholder review
- [ ] Create a risk dashboard template (1 page)
- [ ] Answer: "How often should a risk register be updated? What happens if it's not?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Update your live RAID Log (Week 8 running)
- [ ] Compare approaches: Risk Management from Deep Dive vs. Risk Dependency Guide
- [ ] **Action:** Add a risk dashboard sample to your portfolio

---

### Week 9: Six Core Competencies - Cross-Functional Communications & Delivery

**Goal:** Deep understanding of the fourth and fifth competencies.

**Day 1 - Cross-Functional Communication Systems (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "Cross-Functional Communications" section from 03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md
- [ ] Study the Pulse Financial case study in detail
- [ ] Design a communication matrix for a 4-team program

**Day 2 - Communication Artifacts (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Create: RAG status report template, decision log template, meeting notes template
- [ ] Practice: Write a "1-3-1" communication for a real issue
- [ ] Answer: "What's the difference between synchronous and asynchronous communication? When should you use each?"

**Day 3 - Delivery & Execution Systems (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Delivery & Execution" from 03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md
- [ ] Study the GreenLeaf Energy case study in detail
- [ ] Practice: Design a Definition of Done (4 levels) for a program

**Day 4 - Delivery Cadence & Launch (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Design a delivery cadence: daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly rhythms
- [ ] Create a launch checklist (pre-launch, launch day, post-launch)
- [ ] Practice: "What do you do when you discover at Week 7 that integration testing will take 6 weeks?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Update your live RAID Log
- [ ] Practice: Deliver a 3-minute "program status update" on camera
- [ ] **Action:** Add your communication templates to your portfolio

---

### Week 10: Competency 6 & Technical Depth - Influence Without Authority

**Goal:** Deep understanding of the sixth competency and technical foundations.

**Day 1 - Influence Without Authority (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Influence Without Authority" from 03_TPM_Six_Core_Competencies_Deep_Dive.md (file may be truncated - read what's available)
- [ ] Also read the Negotiation section from 07_TPM_Stakeholder_Communication_Guide.md
- [ ] Answer: "How do you get someone to do something when you're not their manager?"

**Day 2 - Technical Depth: System Design (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "System Design" from `09_TPM_Technical_Depth_Research_Report.md`
- [ ] Understand: Foundational vs. Intermediate vs. Advanced levels
- [ ] Practice: Identify when a microservices architecture is over-engineered
- [ ] Review the fintech case study about the fraud detection system

**Day 3 - Technical Depth: APIs & CI/CD (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "APIs" and "CI/CD Pipelines" from 09_TPM_Technical_Depth_Research_Report.md
- [ ] For APIs: Memorize versioning strategies and breaking change patterns
- [ ] For CI/CD: Understand blue-green, canary, and feature flag deployments
- [ ] Practice: Design an API contract review process

**Day 4 - Technical Depth: Architecture Reviews (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "Architecture Reviews" from 09_TPM_Technical_Depth_Research_Report.md
- [ ] Understand: What TPMs evaluate vs. what they defer to architects
- [ ] Practice: "You're in an architecture review. The architect proposes 15 microservices. Your team has 12 people. What do you say?"
- [ ] Review the health-tech case study about the simplified architecture

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read the "Cross-Domain Synthesis" section of 09_TPM_Technical_Depth_Research_Report.md
- [ ] Identify: Which domains need Intermediate depth vs. Foundational only
- [ ] Create a personal learning plan: "Which technical domain should I prioritize next?"
- [ ] **Action:** Update your portfolio with architecture review learnings

---

### Week 11: Metrics & Execution Tracking

**Goal:** Master the metrics that TPMs use to track program health.

**Day 1 - KPIs & OKR Tracking (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Read "KPIs" and "OKR Tracking" from `06_TPM_Metrics_Execution_Tracking.md`
- [ ] Practice: Define 7 KPIs for a hypothetical program
- [ ] Practice: Set up an OKR tracking dashboard with confidence scoring
- [ ] Answer: "What's the difference between a leading indicator and a lagging indicator?"

### Week 17: Deep Practice - Program Design Cases

**Goal:** Master the program design (case study) interview format.

**Day 1 - Framework Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Re-read the Program Design section from `10_TPM_Interview_Prep_Guide.md` (6-Step Framework)
- [ ] Memorize the 6 steps: Clarify → Structure → Plan → Identify Risks → Communicate → Launch
- [ ] Practice saying the framework aloud 3 times

**Day 2 - Practice Case 1 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Prompt: "Your company is launching a new mobile app feature across 4 teams. You have 6 weeks. Walk me through your plan."
- [ ] Work through all 6 steps out loud (record on camera - 10 minutes)
- [ ] Watch it back. Note: Did you clarify before jumping to solutions? Did you identify risks?

**Day 3 - Practice Case 2 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Prompt: "A critical third-party API your program depends on is being deprecated. You have 8 weeks to migrate. What do you do?"
- [ ] Work through all 6 steps out loud (record on camera - 10 minutes)
- [ ] Watch it back. Note: Did you mention escalation? Rollback plan?

**Day 4 - Practice Case 3 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Prompt: "Your program is 6 months in and 2 months behind. Stakeholders want to know the recovery plan. What do you tell them?"
- [ ] Work through all 6 steps out loud (record on camera - 10 minutes)
- [ ] Watch it back. Note: How did you handle the bad news? Buffer? Tradeoffs?

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Practice: Give yourself a random prompt, answer in 10 minutes (record)
- [ ] Review all 3 recordings - identify 1 thing to improve for each
- [ ] **Action:** You now have 3 practiced case study answers

### Week 18: Deep Practice - Estimation Questions

**Goal:** Master the estimation question format.

**Day 1 - Framework Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Re-read the Estimation section from `10_TPM_Interview_Prep_Guide.md` (5-Step Framework)
- [ ] Memorize: Clarify → Break Down → State Assumptions → Math Out Loud → Sanity Check
- [ ] Practice the framework with a simple question: "How many WhatsApp messages are sent in India daily?"

**Day 2 - Practice Estimation 1 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Prompt: "How many active software developers are there in India?"
- [ ] Work through all 5 steps out loud (record - 5 minutes)
- [ ] Check your answer against the worked example in the Interview Prep Guide

**Day 3 - Practice Estimation 2 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Prompt: "How long would it take to build a simple e-commerce checkout page?"
- [ ] Work through all 5 steps out loud (record - 5 minutes)
- [ ] Check against the worked example

**Day 4 - Practice Estimation 3 & 4 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Prompt: "How long would it take to migrate a 50-table database from PostgreSQL to MongoDB?"
- [ ] Prompt: "Estimate the annual cloud compute cost for a startup with 100,000 users."
- [ ] Work through both (record each - 5 minutes)

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Review all estimation recordings
- [ ] Key insight: Interviewers care about your *reasoning*, not the *number*
- [ ] Practice: "If my assumption about X is wrong, the estimate would be Y instead" - shows flexibility
- [ ] **Action:** You now have 5 practiced estimation answers

### Week 21: Mock Interviews - Self-Run

**Goal:** Simulate real interview conditions.

**Day 1 - Full Mock 1 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Set a timer for 45 minutes
- [ ] Answer these questions in sequence (record):
  1. "Tell me about yourself" (2 min)
  2. "Why TPM?" (2 min)
  3. "Tell me about a complex program you managed" (use Sally - 3 min)
  4. "How do you handle a stakeholder who keeps changing scope?" (3 min)
  5. "Walk me through how you'd plan a 6-month program with 4 teams" (5 min)
  6. "Do you have any questions for me?" (3 min)
- [ ] Watch the entire recording. Note patterns: Do you ramble? Use filler words? Lose structure?

**Day 2 - Full Mock 2 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Different set of questions (record):
  1. "Tell me about yourself" (2 min)
  2. "Why this company?" (2 min)
  3. "Tell me about a time you led without authority" (use Manager Absence - 3 min)
  4. "Describe a conflict you resolved between teams" (use UI Overhaul - 3 min)
  5. "Your eng lead says 6 weeks, stakeholders need 4. How do you handle it?" (3 min)
  6. "How do you keep executives informed?" (3 min)
  7. "Questions for me?" (3 min)
- [ ] Watch and note improvements

**Day 3 - Estimation & Case Study Mock (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Practice estimation: "How many Uber rides happen in Bangalore daily?" (5 min)
- [ ] Practice program design: "We're launching a new payments feature across 5 teams in 3 months. Walk me through your approach." (10 min)
- [ ] Record both - check for framework usage

**Day 4 - Weakness Focus (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Identify: Which question type was weakest in your mocks?
- [ ] Practice that specific type 5 times
- [ ] Re-record the full mock that included that question

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Review best and worst moments from all mocks
- [ ] Write down: "My 3 strengths in interviews are... My 2 areas to improve are..."
- [ ] **Action:** Schedule your first real peer mock interview for Week 22

### Week 22: Mock Interviews - With Real People

**Goal:** Get external feedback on your interview performance.

**Day 1 - Prepare for Peer Mock (2 hrs)**

- [ ] Prepare a briefing document for your mock partner: your background, target roles, what feedback you want

**Day 2 - Peer Mock 1 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Conduct a 30-minute mock interview with a TPM connection

- [ ] Write down EVERYTHING they said (you'll forget if you don't)
- [ ] Practice the areas they flagged for improvement

**Day 3 - Peer Mock 2 (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Conduct a second 30-minute mock with a different person
- [ ] Ask the same questions, get different perspectives
- [ ] Write down everything
- [ ] Compare: Is the feedback consistent across both mocks?

**Day 4 - Apply Feedback (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Re-record all answers that received constructive feedback
- [ ] Practice the improved versions 3 times each
- [ ] Answer: "What did I learn from real interviewers that I couldn't learn from a mirror?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Update your STAR stories based on feedback
- [ ] Update your frameworks based on feedback
- [ ] **Action:** Apply to 10 jobs this week (see v5.md Section 6 for target companies)

**Goal:** Navigate real interviews and be ready to negotiate.

**Day 1 - Interview Prep Checklist (2 hrs)**

- [ ] Learn: ESOP evaluation framework (v5.md Section 10)
- [ ] Practice: "Thank you, I'd like to review the offer in writing. I'll get back to you in 2 business days."
- [ ] Practice the negotiation script (v5.md Section 10)

**Day 4 - Interview Debrief Practice (2 hrs)**
- [ ] Create an interview debrief template (Company, Round, Questions, My Answers, Strengths, Weaknesses)
- [ ] Practice writing a debrief for a hypothetical interview
- [ ] Answer: "If an interview went badly, what's my recovery plan? How do I improve for the next one?"

**Day 5 - Weekly Review (2 hrs)**

- [ ] Apply to 5 more jobs (total this week: 15)

It is: > **"I lead the delivery of complex AI and data-platform initiatives involving multiple stakeholders, dependencies, and business outcomes."** Your technical expertise exists in four distinct pillars: | Pillar | Stack | |--------|-------| | **Frontend** | React.js, Redux, TypeScript, D3.js, Enterprise UI Systems | | **Backend** | Python, FastAPI, API Design | | **AI / GenAI** | RAG, GraphRAG, LLM Applications, Agent Systems, Vector Search, Enterprise Copilots | | **Data Platforms** | Neo4j, Knowledge Graphs, Semantic Search, Data Lineage | **Interview usage:** Most TPM candidates have zero AI/GenAI delivery experience. You have two flagship case studies in it (Sally, CDL). Lead with this in every application to fintech + tech-forward companies. This is a 2026 differentiator that most SWE-to-TPM candidates cannot match. --- ## 2. Phase 1 - TPM Foundation (Months 1–3) ### ⚡ Two Tracks. Run Both in Parallel. The default TPM transition roadmap looks like this: ``` Learn → Apply → Get TPM Job ``` That produces candidates who say: *"I've been studying RAID logs."* This roadmap adds a second track running **from this Monday**: ``` Learn → Start Running TPM Processes at Relevance Lab → Collect Real Evidence → Apply ``` That produces practitioners who say: *"I maintain a live RAID log for a 4-team program at Relevance Lab. Here's a blocker I cleared with it last sprint."* Interviewers cannot dispute lived evidence. A certificate is a credential. Evidence is proof. --- ### Internal TPM Conversion Track - Start This Monday You don't need a new job to start doing TPM work. You need to apply TPM processes to work you're **already doing** at Relevance Lab - Sally, CDL, or any active sprint. No one's permission required. You're a Senior Engineer. Just start. #### The Three Actions - This Week **Action 1 - Create a RAID Log for Sally (30 minutes)** Open Section 18.2. Fill it with Sally's current state: every dependency, every blocker, every assumption being made. Maintain it going forward. Bring it up in your next standup as "I've been maintaining a dependency and risk tracker - here's the current state." Most engineers don't do this. You now do. **Action 2 - Send One Executive Update This Week (20 minutes)** Use Section 18.6. Write a one-pager for Sally or CDL. Send it to your manager or team lead. You don't need to wait to be asked - send it and say: *"I've been working on structured program communication - here's a status update for Sally."* This single email creates written evidence that someone received and acknowledged your TPM work. **Action 3 - Run a Dependency Tracker for Your Current Sprint (20 minutes)** Open Section 18.3. Fill it in for any cross-team dependencies in your current sprint - Frontend/Backend, Infra, QA, whatever exists right now. Share it in the next standup as a living doc. This takes 20 minutes to create and is indefinitely maintainable. #### Evidence Collection - Do This Weekly Start a **Wins Journal** - a Google Doc or Notion page, private, dated entries: ``` Date: [YYYY-MM-DD] Program: Sally / CDL / Other Action: [What you did - RAID log updated, status report sent, blocker escalated] Outcome: [What changed - dependency unblocked, decision made, delay prevented] ``` This journal directly feeds: Section 19 case study data, resume bullets, STAR stories, and your Month 12 performance review. Start it today, not after you get the job. #### What You Can Say in Interviews by Month 3 > *"For the past 3 months, I've been applying TPM processes to my current programs at Relevance Lab - maintaining a RAID log, sending weekly RAG status reports to stakeholders, and tracking cross-team dependencies. I didn't wait for the title to start behaving like a TPM."* That single statement separates you from 90% of SWE-to-TPM candidates who are still in learning mode. | Without this track | With this track | |--------------------|-----------------| | *"I've been studying RAID logs."* | *"I maintain a live RAID log for the Sally program - want to see the format I use?"* | | *"I understand stakeholder communication."* | *"I've been sending weekly RAG status reports at Relevance Lab since [month]."* | | *"I know how to handle dependencies."* | *"I tracked and cleared 3 cross-team dependencies in CDL last sprint."* | | *"I'm learning program management."* | *"I'm already doing it - I'm here for the official title."* | --- ### Month 1 - Build the Foundation #### Week 1–2: Understand the TPM Role **What a TPM actually does (internalize this):** - Owns the *delivery* of a technical program, not the code - Coordinates across engineering, product, design, QA, ops - Manages dependencies, risks, timelines, and blockers - Communicates status upward (execs) and sideways (teams) - Does NOT write code day-to-day **Free study resources (2 hrs/day):** - Atlassian: "What is a Technical Program Manager?" (atlassian.com) - YouTube search: "TPM day in the life Google" / "TPM vs PM vs EM" - Glassdoor: Read TPM interview reviews at Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Flipkart - Read 5 TPM job descriptions daily - note recurring keywords **Week 1–2 goal:** Write a 1-page document: "What a TPM does and why I'm a fit." --- #### Week 3–4: Learn Core TPM Frameworks **Agile / Scrum (mandatory):** - Scrum.org: free resources + Scrum Guide PDF (the actual standard) - Understand: sprints, backlog, standups, retrospectives, velocity, burndown - Know the difference: Scrum vs Kanban vs SAFe **Program Management fundamentals:** - OKRs - how to write and track Objectives & Key Results - RACI matrix - Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed - RAID log - Risks, Assumptions, Issues, Dependencies - Critical path method - which tasks block others - Milestone planning vs task planning **Tools (you already know Jira - go deeper):** - Jira: epics, stories, subtasks, sprint boards, dashboards, filters - Confluence: meeting notes, decision logs, runbooks - Notion: docs, program wikis, meeting notes (widely used at Indian product startups - free personal plan) - Miro/FigJam: dependency mapping (free tier) - Google Sheets: program timeline / Gantt chart (use this heavily in interviews) **Free courses:** - Coursera: Google Project Management Certificate - audit free (no certificate) - Atlassian University: Free Jira + Confluence training (university.atlassian.com) - Scrum.org: Free learning path --- #### Month 2 - Build TPM Artifacts & Start Networking **Week 5–6: Create a TPM Portfolio** You don't have the title, but you have the work. Document it like a TPM would. **Portfolio Artifact 1 - Relevance Lab UI Overhaul:** ``` Program: Frontend Overhaul Delivery Scope: ReactJS migration/redesign across 4-person team My role: Coordination, dependency tracking, conflict resolution Challenges: [specific blockers you dealt with] Outcome: Delivered on schedule. Resolved X conflicts. Reduced rework. Tools used: Jira, Git, Slack ``` **Portfolio Artifact 2 - Manager Absence Coverage:** ``` Program: Team continuity during manager's absence Scope: Owned daily standups, stakeholder communication, priority calls My role: Acted as program lead for N weeks Challenges: Unblocking team decisions without authority Outcome: Zero delivery slippage. Team morale maintained. ``` **Portfolio Artifact 3 - Junior Developer Mentoring Program (frame it as a program):** ``` Initiative: Developer Onboarding & Mentorship Scope: Mentored 2 junior developers Structure: Weekly 1:1s, code review process, ramp-up timeline Outcome: Both devs became independent contributors within [X weeks] ``` > Store these as PDFs or a simple portfolio site (GitHub Pages, free). **Week 7–8: Cross-Team Coordination Documentation** - Write a "lessons learned" doc from any cross-team coordination work you did - **Your RAID log for Sally/CDL should be 4+ weeks old by now** - document it as a real artifact, not a sample. Export it as a PDF for your portfolio. This is your evidence. - Export your Dependency Tracker as a screenshot or PDF - this also goes in your portfolio - Create a sample program timeline in Google Sheets (3 teams, 8 weeks, milestones) - still useful for interview case studies even though your RAID log is now real --- #### Month 3 - Apply, Practice, Submit See dedicated sections: [Resume](#resume), [LinkedIn](#linkedin), [Interview Prep](#interviewprep), [Job Search](#jobsearch) **Month 3 checklist:** - [ ] Resume final version ready (ATS + human optimized) - [ ] LinkedIn fully updated - [ ] 20 cold outreach messages sent - [ ] 30 jobs applied (rolling basis) - [ ] 10 TPM interview questions practiced on camera - [ ] 5 STAR stories memorized - [ ] RAID log for Sally/CDL has been running for 8+ weeks ✅ - [ ] At least 2 Executive Updates sent to stakeholders ✅ - [ ] Wins Journal has 8+ entries with dates and outcomes ✅ - [ ] Ready to say in interviews: *"I'm already doing TPM work - I'm here for the title."* --- ## 3. LinkedIn Strategy - Complete Overhaul LinkedIn is your #1 tool. Recruiters search it. TPMs use it. You'll get referrals through it. ### Step 1: Profile Photo & Banner - Photo: Clean background, professional but not stiff. Smile. - Banner: Use Canva (free) to create a banner that says something like: `"Software Engineer → Technical Program Manager | MBA | ReactJS | Agile"` Dark background, minimal text, readable. --- ### Step 2: Headline (most important field) **Bad headline:** `Software Engineer at Relevance Lab` **Good headlines - pick one:** ``` Option A: Software Engineer → Technical Program Manager | Cross-team Coordination | Agile | MBA Candidate Option B: TPM Aspirant | 4.5 Yrs Engineering @ Relevance Lab | Agile · Stakeholder Mgmt · Conflict Resolution Option C: Technical Program Manager (Transitioning) | ReactJS · Python · Jira | Open to Relocation ``` Keywords recruiters search: Technical Program Manager, TPM, Agile, Scrum, Cross-functional, Stakeholder Management, Program Delivery --- ### Step 3: About Section ``` I'm a Senior Software Engineer with 4.5 years of experience at Relevance Lab, actively transitioning into Technical Program Management. Over my engineering career, I've naturally gravitated toward coordination, delivery ownership, and cross-functional leadership - not just code. I've driven two significant programs end-to-end: Sally (enterprise AI assistant) and CDL (GenAI data lineage platform), coordinating multiple teams, managing dependencies, and owning delivery outcomes on both. My strengths: → AI/GenAI program delivery - Sally + CDL are two flagship programs in production AI systems → Cross-team coordination (handled multi-team delivery as informal TPM) → Conflict resolution & stakeholder communication (8–9/10 self-assessment) → Technical depth (React, TypeScript, Python, FastAPI, Neo4j) - I understand what engineers build → Leadership - mentored junior developers from onboarding to independence Currently pursuing an MBA (3rd semester), which is strengthening my business acumen and strategic thinking. I'm looking for a TPM role where I can own program delivery, lead cross-functional initiatives, and grow toward a Technical Director / VP track. Open to: India (any city) | Global remote Reach me: [your email] ``` --- ### Step 4: Experience Section - Reframe Everything **DO NOT write:** "Developed ReactJS components for client-facing dashboards" **DO write:** ``` Technical Lead & Cross-Team Coordinator | Relevance Lab | [dates] → Served as cross-functional coordinator across 2–3 teams for complex delivery initiatives, managing dependencies and resolving blockers to ensure on-time delivery. → Drove UI overhaul delivery across a 4-person engineering team - owned timeline, coordinated daily standups, resolved inter-team conflicts. → Owned program continuity during manager's absence - led team syncs, made priority decisions, maintained stakeholder communication with zero slippage. → Mentored 2 junior developers - designed onboarding plan, conducted weekly 1:1s, accelerated ramp-up from 0 to independent contributor in [X weeks]. → Technical stack: ReactJS, Python, Jira, Git, Agile/Scrum ``` > Use numbers wherever possible. Even estimates are fine: "reduced rework by ~30%", "team of 4", "2 junior developers" --- ### Step 5: Skills Section Add all of these - ask connections to endorse: ``` Core: Technical Program Management, Program Delivery, Cross-functional Coordination, Stakeholder Management, Conflict Resolution, Agile, Scrum, Kanban Tools: Jira, Confluence, Git, ReactJS, Python Soft: Leadership, Communication, Problem-solving, Mentoring, Risk Management ``` --- ### Step 6: Featured Section Add 2–3 items: - Link to your GitHub (ORBIS or any strong project - shows technical credibility) - A PDF portfolio doc of your TPM artifacts (the 3 portfolio items from Month 2) - use the **Sally and CDL case studies in Section 19** as your primary portfolio items once filled in - A well-written LinkedIn article (optional but powerful - write "Why I'm transitioning from SWE to TPM") --- ### Step 7: Recommendations **Ask these people for LinkedIn recommendations:** 1. A senior engineer you worked with - ask them to mention your coordination/leadership 2. A junior developer you mentored - ask them to mention your mentoring impact 3. Anyone who saw you as the cross-team coordinator / team lead during manager's absence **Template to send:** ``` Hi [Name], I'm transitioning into TPM roles and am updating my LinkedIn profile. Would you be comfortable writing a short recommendation highlighting my [coordination / mentoring / leadership] skills from our time at Relevance Lab? Even 2–3 sentences would mean a lot. Happy to return the favor. Thanks! ``` --- ### Step 8: LinkedIn Activity Strategy **Consistency beats virality. Post 2x/week:** | Post Type | Example | |-----------|---------| | Learning share | "Today I learned what a RAID log is and why every TPM needs one. Here's a breakdown..." | | Career story | "Why I'm transitioning from Software Engineer to TPM - and what that looks like" | | Insight post | "What's the difference between a TPM and a PM? I was confused too. Here's what I found..." | | Repost + comment | Share a TPM's post with your perspective added | **Engagement (15 min/day):** - Comment on 3–5 TPM posts daily with thoughtful responses (not just "great post!") - This puts your name in front of TPMs and recruiters organically **LinkedIn Premium:** - Worth it for 1 month when you're actively applying (InMail credits) - Use the free trial first --- ### Step 9: Recruiter Outreach on LinkedIn **Search query:** `"Technical Program Manager" "India" recruiter` OR filter by: HR Manager + target company **Message template:** ``` Hi [Name], I'm a Senior Software Engineer with 4.5 years of experience transitioning into TPM roles. I noticed you recruit for technical roles at [Company]. Would you be open to a quick conversation about any TPM openings, or share who I should connect with? Thank you - Biren ``` --- ## 4. Resume Strategy - Complete ### ATS Optimization (Critical - most resumes die here) Companies use Applicant Tracking Systems that scan for keywords before a human ever sees your resume. **ATS rules:** - Use a clean, single-column format (no tables, columns, graphics) - Use standard section names: "Work Experience", "Skills", "Education" - Include exact keywords from job descriptions - Submit as PDF (unless .docx is requested) - No headers/footers with important info - ATS ignores them **ATS keyword bank for TPM (use these throughout):** ``` Technical Program Manager, Program Management, Cross-functional coordination, Stakeholder management, Agile, Scrum, Sprint planning, Backlog grooming, Risk management, RAID log, Dependency tracking, Roadmap planning, OKRs, KPIs, Jira, Confluence, Milestone planning, Delivery management, Conflict resolution, Escalation management, Executive communication ``` --- ### Resume Format ``` [Name] [Phone] | [Email] | [LinkedIn URL] | [Location - Bihar, IN | Open to relocate] SUMMARY Senior Software Engineer with 4.5 years of experience, transitioning to Technical Program Management. Delivered two end-to-end AI/GenAI programs (Sally - enterprise AI assistant; CDL - GenAI data lineage platform) as cross-functional delivery coordinator across multiple teams. Strong background in stakeholder management, dependency tracking, and delivery ownership. Proven leadership: mentored junior developers, owned program continuity during management absence. MBA candidate (3rd semester). Seeking TPM role to own complex technical programs. CORE COMPETENCIES Cross-team Coordination | Stakeholder Management | Agile/Scrum | Program Delivery | Risk Management Conflict Resolution | Dependency Tracking | AI/GenAI Program Delivery | Mentoring | React | Python | Neo4j PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE Senior Software Engineer → Cross-team Delivery Coordinator | Relevance Lab | [Start] – Present → Drove end-to-end delivery of Sally (enterprise AI assistant) - coordinated Frontend, Backend, Infra, and AI/ML workstreams; managed dependencies, resolved blockers, maintained stakeholder visibility. → Drove end-to-end delivery of CDL (GenAI data lineage platform) - coordinated GraphRAG pipeline, Knowledge Graph build, and D3.js visualization workstreams across multiple teams. → Coordinated multi-team deliveries across 3+ teams as designated cross-team coordinator, managing dependencies and resolving blockers to maintain schedule adherence. → Maintained program continuity during manager's 4-week absence - led team syncs, owned stakeholder updates, and made priority decisions with zero delivery slippage. → Designed and executed a structured onboarding program for 2 junior developers, reducing time-to-independence from industry-average 3 months to [X weeks]. → Championed Agile practices within the team - introduced structured retrospectives that reduced repeat blockers by ~25%. EDUCATION MBA (ongoing) | [University] | 2023 – Present | 3rd Semester BBA(Computer Application) | [Branch] | [University] | [Year] SKILLS Program Management: Agile, Scrum, Jira, Confluence, RAID Logs, OKRs, Sprint Planning Technical: ReactJS, Python, Git, REST APIs Tools: Jira, Confluence, Google Sheets, Slack, GitHub PROJECTS / PORTFOLIO (optional - add if space allows) → TPM Portfolio: Documented 3 program management artifacts (available on request) → ORBIS Trading Platform: Full-stack system (FastAPI + React) - demonstrates technical depth and end-to-end product delivery capability ``` --- ### Resume Do's and Don'ts | Do | Don't | |----|-------| | Lead every bullet with a strong verb: Led, Owned, Coordinated, Drove, Managed | "Responsible for" or "Helped with" | | Use numbers: "4-person team", "3 teams", "2 junior devs", "0 delivery slippage" | Vague claims with no proof | | Tailor for each company (swap in their keywords) | Submit the same resume to all | | Keep to 1 page (you have <5 years experience) | 2+ pages | | Put your MBA in Education even though ongoing | Hide it | | Have someone else proofread it | Submit with typos | --- ## 5. Networking & Cold Outreach ### Who to Reach Out To **Priority 1: TPMs at your target companies** - Search LinkedIn: `"Technical Program Manager" + "Google" / "Amazon" / "Razorpay"` - Filter: India-based, 3–8 years experience (closest to your level) - Goal: 5 messages/day for 4 weeks = ~140 connections **Priority 2: Engineers who became TPMs** - Search: `"Former Software Engineer" "Technical Program Manager"` - These people understand your exact journey - highest response rate **Priority 3: Recruiters at target companies** - Search: `"recruiter" "technical" "Google India"` etc. - One message → potential fast-track to interview --- ### Cold Outreach Templates **Template A - For TPMs (info request):** ``` Hi [Name], I noticed your journey from [engineering/similar background] to TPM at [Company] - that's exactly the path I'm working toward. I'm a software engineer with 4.5 years experience and strong cross-team coordination skills. Would you have 10 minutes for a virtual coffee? No ask, just curious about your transition story and any advice you'd share. Thanks - Biren ``` **Template B - For TPMs who posted content:** ``` Hi [Name], Your post on [specific topic] really clicked for me - especially the point about [specific thing]. I'm transitioning from SWE to TPM and that framing helped. Would love to connect and occasionally learn from your perspective if that's okay. - Biren ``` **Template C - For recruiters:** ``` Hi [Name], I'm a software engineer with 4.5 years of experience actively transitioning into Technical Program Management. I've been building coordination, stakeholder management, and delivery ownership experience at Relevance Lab and would love to explore TPM opportunities at [Company]. Is there a TPM opening I should apply to, or a better person to connect with? Thank you - Biren ``` **Template D - Referral ask (after 1–2 conversations):** ``` Hi [Name], Really appreciated our chat last week. Your advice on [X] was genuinely helpful. I'm actively applying for TPM roles and noticed [Company] has an opening. If you're comfortable and it's a fit, would you be open to referring me internally? I'll make sure my application is strong before I ask you to put your name on it. No pressure either way - Biren ``` --- ### Follow-Up Cadence | Trigger | When | What to Say | |---------|------|-------------| | No reply to connection request | 7 days | Send a short follow-up message | | Had a chat, no follow-up | After 1 week | "Thanks again for your time - one quick question..." | | Applied, no update in 2 weeks | Week 3 | Message recruiter on LinkedIn for status | | Referral pending | After 10 days | "Just checking in - happy to send over my resume if helpful" | --- ## 5A. MBA Leverage Strategy Your MBA is more than a credential on your resume - it's an active tool most TPM candidates leave completely unused. Here's how to extract full value from it. ### Networking via MBA Network **Batch-mates in PM/TPM/product roles:** Your highest-conversion referral pool. Send a message saying you're transitioning and ask for referrals or intros. People refer batch-mates more readily than LinkedIn strangers. Start with a LinkedIn search: `[Your MBA university] alumni → filter by "Technical Program Manager" or "Product Manager" → India`. **Alumni at target companies:** Use your university's LinkedIn alumni tab. One warm alumni intro beats 30 cold outreach messages. This is especially powerful for Razorpay, Freshworks, PhonePe - companies that actively track MBA talent. **University career services:** Many online MBA programs have placement partnerships with companies, or at minimum a job board. Check yours - some run hiring cycles specifically for MBA students. ### Portfolio Leverage - **Frame MBA coursework as program delivery:** A group project is a multi-stakeholder program. An operations management assignment is a program planning exercise. Document your best ones like a TPM would and include them in your portfolio. - **Use electives strategically:** If you have elective choices remaining, prioritize Operations Management, Project Management, and Technology Strategy - these directly feed your TPM narrative. - **MBA capstone/thesis:** If you have one upcoming, align the topic to technology program management (e.g., "Agile adoption in Indian fintech startups" or "Program delivery frameworks for AI product teams"). This becomes a portfolio centerpiece. ### In Interviews - **Weave MBA concepts naturally:** "One framework I've been building on from my MBA is..." - this demonstrates business thinking alongside execution capability. - **Use MBA as proof of career intent:** "I'm not randomly pivoting - I'm building this transition deliberately: MBA for strategic thinking, TPM portfolio for delivery credibility, and 4.5 years of technical depth as the foundation." This directly neutralizes the "are you sure about this?" concern. - **Finance + strategy literacy:** When asked about prioritization or tradeoffs, referencing ROI, NPV, or cost of delay correctly signals that you think like a business leader - rare in engineering-to-TPM candidates. ### Long-Term: MBA + TPM = TPdM Track The MBA + engineering + TPM combination is exactly the profile companies recruit for at the Senior TPdM and Director level. Position this as your long-term differentiation from Month 1, not just when you're interviewing for TPdM roles at Month 18. --- ## 6.
